FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study participants (N=10,691; 5,107 girls) with baseline structural imaging data from U.S. cohort

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) = qualifying smartphone variable; total screen time used as predictor in linear mixed-effect models (B1 rule applies)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effect models testing associations between structural covariation pattern and SMA; also tested associations with psychopathology and cognitive measures via regression

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression β coefficients reported: SMA association β = 0.107, P = 0.002; externalizing psychopathology association β = 0.117, P = 0.002

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis using JIVE approach to identify structural covariation patterns and linear mixed-effect regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Full peer-reviewed journal article; no evidence of preprint/abstract/deposit duplication

FT_EC7_reason: SMA is an independent variable predicting brain structural covariation and externalizing behavior outcomes. This is SM→outcome analysis, not demographic→SM analysis. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria (a).
notes: SMA defined as total screen time; analyses cross-sectional at baseline ABCD visit

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study baseline data (N=11,834 children ages 9-10 from U.S. cohort)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time activity = qualifying smartphone variable tested in mixed-effects model predicting general p factor (B1 rule applies); separate analysis includes cognitive performance associations

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects model predicting p factor from screen time and other variables; multiple linear regression tests; CFA with inferential fit indices; t-tests comparing diagnostic groups

FT_IC4.evidence: Variance explained by mixed-effects model = 8.02%; correlation coefficients r=0.87 (p factor models), r=0.32 (internalizing), r=0.02 (externalizing); CFA fit indices (CFI=0.957/0.948, TLI=0.928/0.927, RMSEA=0.112/0.060)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis using CFA bifactor modeling, mixed-effects regression, and diagnostic group comparisons

FT_IC6.evidence: Full peer-reviewed conference paper (IEEE BIBM 2021); no evidence of duplication

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is IV in mixed-effects model predicting p factor (outcome), combined with non-demographic behavioral predictors (sleep duration, physical activity) and demographics (income, gender). Additional non-demographic variables disqualify per B17 (sleep is excluded from demographic-only set). Analysis is cross-sectional, not longitudinal.
notes: Cross-sectional baseline analysis; screen time variable from ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey

FT_IC1.evidence: Describes ABCD study design and participants (nearly 12,000 youth aged 9-10 from U.S.)

FT_IC2.evidence: Perspective/commentary paper describing ABCD measures including 'screen media use' but does NOT report any original empirical analysis testing SM/smartphone as exposure variable with outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential statistical tests reported for any SM associations; article is descriptive overview of ABCD study

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric coefficients (β, OR, r, p-values) extracted for SM associations; only descriptive statistics about ABCD sample composition

FT_IC5.evidence: Perspective/commentary article describing ABCD study opportunities (FT-EC3 applies: not original empirical analysis; meets B14 criteria as resource/data paper)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published commentary; no evidence of duplication

FT_EC7_reason: Paper does not meet FT-IC5 (original empirical analysis requirement); therefore FT-EC7 is not applicable. This is a perspective/commentary describing study design and measures, not reporting analyses.
notes: Does not qualify for inclusion; resource/description paper only. Table 1 lists screen time monitoring as a measure but no empirical analysis is reported.
